step_by_step_reasoning: We identify the nodes on the whiteboard: an EKS cluster and EC2 instances inside a VPC utilizing a 'reused' CIDR space. In the same VPC, NAT Gateway (NGW) and Network Load Balancer (NLB) are deployed in a 'routed' CIDR space. Transit Gateway serves as the regional hub, connecting the VPC to Direct Connect, VPN, and a Shared Services VPC via Private Link. We map these exact components to standard AWS nodes, preserving the flow from EKS -> NGW -> Transit Gateway, and Transit Gateway -> Direct Connect / VPN / Private Link.

graph.name: Adobe Scalable Regional VPC Architecture
graph.notes: Adobe leverages Transit Gateway to handle networking at scale across thousands of AWS accounts, using a dual CIDR block strategy (reused and routed space) to optimize IPv4 addresses.

nodes.name: EKS Cluster
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Hosts product workloads inside the reused CIDR block.

nodes.name: EC2 Instances
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Runs customer applications alongside EKS in the reused CIDR block.

nodes.name: NGW
nodes.notes: DATA_PEEK: Translates local EKS/EC2 IPs to enable external and cross-VPC communication.

nodes.name: NLB
nodes.notes: DATA_PEEK: Handles inbound traffic to the customer VPC application endpoints.

nodes.name: TRANSIT GW
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Acts as regional hub routing traffic between VPCs and on-premise networks.

nodes.name: DIRECT CONNECT
nodes.notes: DATA_PEEK: Hybrid cloud link to on-premise infrastructure.

nodes.name: VPC PRIVATE LINK
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Centralizes access to shared services securely across the network.

edges.notes: Outbound traffic from reused subnet routes to NAT Gateway.
NAT Gateway forwards traffic out to Transit Gateway.
Transit Gateway routes data to on-premise via Direct Connect.
Transit Gateway routes backup traffic via VPN.
Transit Gateway connects regional VPCs to shared services through Private Link.
